text: During the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy, the surgeon elevates the transverse colon and transverse mesocolon to expose the middle colic artery. The small bowel is pushed to the left lower quadrant by tilting the table, revealing the retroperitoneum and gaining access to the superior mesenteric vessel axis from the duodenojejunal junction to the ileocecal junction.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic trunk and its junction with the superior mesenteric vessels by elevating the ileocolic junction, creating a visible groove between these vessels. A lymph node is observed in this area. The peritoneum is opened, allowing gas to dissect the plane, aiming to expose the duodenum for dissection from the mesocolon and the back of the ileocolic trunk to safely ligate the trunk.

The surgeon uses a grasper to elevate the ileocolic vessels, ligating them flush with their origin from the superior mesenteric vessels. The duodenum is visible, safely dissected downward. The ileocolic vein and artery are ligated at their origin, with lymph nodes and lymphatics included in the specimen.

The surgeon applies upward traction on the ileocolic vessel stump to develop the medial to lateral plane. Dissection proceeds between Toldt's fascia and the mesocolon, moving downwards towards the iliopsoas muscle. The iliopsoas muscle is visible, marking the end of the lateral dissection at the lateral parabolic gutter, with retroperitoneal structures protected beneath the fascia.

The surgeon demonstrates the retroperitoneal structures enclosed within Thoth's fascia, highlighting the dissection plane. The dissection progresses cephalad beneath the colon, revealing the peritoneum superior to the colon and underneath the liver. The peritoneum is opened from below to complete the dissection of the colon and its mesentery.

The panoramic view shows the entire colon and its mesentery mobilized from medial to lateral, with only the lateral paracolic attachments remaining. The retroperitoneum has been safely dissected away.

The surgeon continues dissection upwards in the area of the duodenum and head of the pancreas, carefully separating the head of the pancreas from the mesocolon to avoid injury. The head of the pancreas is mobilized downwards to join the duodenum, exposing the right colic vessels. The gallbladder is visible through the peritoneum.

The surgeon displays the ileocolic pedicle, highlighting the complete removal of the mesentery along with all lymph nodes and lymphatics. The ileal mesentery is cut, and the ileum is transected using staplers with white cartridges, potentially requiring one or two applications. This marks the completion of the inferior portion of the surgery, after which the colon is transected, the specimen is removed, and an anastomosis is performed.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization and cuts the paracolic peritoneum, facilitating the removal of the specimen. The transverse colon is brought downwards, and the gastrocolic omentum is opened to separate the colon from the greater omentum, preparing the transverse colon for transection.

The surgical field is shown after specimen removal, revealing the transected colon and its mesentery. The surgeon prepares for an anastomosis by aligning the terminal ileum with the transverse colon, using a stay suture to fix them together. A loose knot is applied, locked, and then unlocked by tumbling to slide the two viscera into close proximity for side-to-side anastomosis in an iso-peristaltic fashion.

The surgeon performs an ileocolic anastomosis using a six-centimeter blue cartridge stapler. The enterotomies are then closed with a running PDS 3-0 suture, ensuring secure closure of the intestinal openings.
this concludes our technique for live image collecting and laparoscopy
